nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral and sublittoral zones of Lake Tanganyika; found among rocky rubble and near sandy substrate, often in areas frequented by mouthbrooding cichlids

decorPreferences: rocky caves and overhangs for hiding
piled rockwork mimicking Tanganyikan littoral zone
open sandy areas between rock formations
smooth substrate — sharp gravel can damage barbels

diet.notes: Opportunistic omnivore; readily accepts sinking pellets, carnivore wafers, frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, krill, and mussel. Also scavenges uneaten food and cichlid eggs. Protein-rich foods support conditioning prior to breeding. Feed after lights-out as the species is nocturnal. Wild fish consume invertebrates, cichlid eggs, and organic detritus.

goodWith: Tanganyikan mouthbrooding cichlids (Tropheus, Cyprichromis, Petrochromis) — for brood-parasitism breeding
other Synodontis species of similar size
avoidWith: small fish that may be swallowed (< 5 cm)
substrate-spawning cichlids whose eggs will be consumed
very small dwarf cichlids that could be harassed
Synodontis petricola in undersized tanks (competition for hiding spots)
non-Tanganyikan species requiring soft acidic water

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image; shows adult coloration — creamy-white body with numerous small dark spots, three pairs of barbels visible.
Wikimedia Commons free-license photograph of adult specimen in aquarium; good lateral view.

summary: The only known fish brood parasite: spawns among mouthbrooding Tanganyikan cichlids, whose hosts unwittingly incubate and raise catfish fry. Robust, hard alkaline water specialist; a striking spotted catfish that also vocalises by stridulation.
careNotes: Synodontis multipunctatus is endemic to Lake Tanganyika and must be kept in hard, alkaline water (pH 7.8–9.0, gH 10–25, kH 8–20) that mirrors its native lake chemistry. Temperature stability in the 23–27°C band is important; unlike many Tanganyikan cichlids it tolerates the slightly wider range seen mid-lake. A minimum 300 L tank with a 150 cm footprint is advisable for a group; the species forms loose social groups and conspecific aggression is moderate — additional hiding places (piled rockwork, ceramic caves) reduce squabbling. Smooth substrate or fine sand protects the delicate barbels. Being nocturnal, it should be fed after lights-out with sinking pellets or carnivore wafers. Do not confuse with Synodontis petricola ('Petricola'), which is smaller (8–10 cm adult), more intensely patterned, and sold as a nano-Tanganyikan catfish — S. multipunctatus reaches 25+ cm and is not suitable for tanks under 300 L. The species produces audible squeaking sounds (stridulation) by rubbing the pectoral-spine base against the pectoral girdle — hence the family common name 'squeaker catfish'. Copper-based medications should be used with caution; scaleless-area sensitivity is moderate. Regular water changes (20–30% weekly) are important to maintain Tanganyikan buffering capacity and low nitrates.
breedingNotes: Synodontis multipunctatus is the only vertebrate known to practise obligate brood parasitism. Spawning is triggered by water changes and the presence of actively spawning mouthbrooding cichlids (particularly Tropheus, Cyprichromis, and Petrochromis). During a cichlid spawning event, one or more catfish rush in to consume the cichlid eggs and simultaneously deposit their own eggs, which the cichlid mother then picks up along with — or instead of — her own. The cichlid incubates the catfish eggs in her buccal cavity; catfish fry hatch before cichlid fry and may consume the remaining cichlid embryos while still in the host's mouth. The adopted fry are released with the cichlid brood. Breeding groups of 4–6 individuals (slight female bias) increase likelihood of spawning. Males are generally slimmer; ripe females become noticeably rounded. Fry accept Artemia nauplii and micro-crushed sinking foods immediately after release. Breeding in a species-only setup is not possible — a mouthbrooding cichlid host species is required. Brood parasitism events are difficult to predict and often happen unnoticed.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis multipunctatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 27.5 cm TL), temperature range (23–27°C), pH (7.8–9.0), hardness requirements, native range (Lake Tanganyika), biotope detail, brood-parasitism breeding biology, diet, and tank requirements. Cross-checked against FishBase and Practical Fishkeeping.

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis multipunctatus
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (max 27.5 cm TL), taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), native range (Lake Tanganyika basin — Burundi, DRC, Tanzania, Zambia), depth range, and diet classification as omnivore with invertebrate and egg predation noted.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Synodontis Catfish Care
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care overview; confirmed hard alkaline water requirements, nocturnal feeding habit, group keeping recommendation, and sinking-food diet. Noted the brood-parasitism behaviour as a key species highlight. Tank size guidance of 75 gal (284 L) for groups aligned with 300 L recommendation used here.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Synodontis multipunctatus vs petricola
additional.notes: Provided detailed comparison between S. multipunctatus (larger, up to 25–27 cm; fewer, more widely spaced spots; three separate dark body stripes as juvenile) and S. petricola (smaller, 8–10 cm adult; denser spotting extending uniformly across body; white-tipped dorsal and caudal fins). Key identification callout used in careNotes.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Cuckoo Catfish: The Brood Parasite of Lake Tanganyika
additional.notes: Detailed account of brood-parasitism mechanism; confirmed that catfish eggs hatch faster than host cichlid eggs and that fry may consume host embryos inside the buccal cavity. Provided spawning-trigger notes (water change + active cichlid spawning). Confirmed stridulation as characteristic of Mochokidae.

fish.breedingNotes: Obligate brood parasite — spawning requires actively spawning mouthbrooding Tanganyikan cichlid hosts (Tropheus, Cyprichromis, Petrochromis). Catfish eggs are picked up by host female and incubated buccally; fry may consume host embryos. Groups of 4–6 catfish recommended; water change triggers spawning. Cannot breed without host cichlids.
